coredata.dc:title: Mechanism of action and cellular responses of HEK293 cells on challenge with zwitterionic carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sangeetha, V.P.
Smriti, Sri
Solanki, Pratima R.
Mohanan, P.V.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon, an extremely versatile element has great demand in the field of nanoscience. Carbon-based nanostructures are exponentially increased due to its wide range of applications in biotechnological and environmental approaches; hence, its safety assessment is of greater concern. In the present study, high quantum yielding zwitterionic carbon dots were synthesized, characterized and its safety assessment at different concentration ranges (50–1600 μgmL−1) on HEK 293 cells was carried out. Cellular, mitochondrial, lysosomal integrity and ROS generation were assessed using specific fluorochromes.The key cellular event apoptosis was assessed by annexinpropidium iodide staining using imaging flow cytometry. Moreover, the mRNA levels of the apoptotic genes were determined by real-time PCR. The results revealed that the cell viability assays (MTT, NR) and mitochondrial membrane potential were altered on exposure to a higher concentration of zwitterionic CDs for 24 h. Also, annexinpropidiumiodidestaining exhibited an increased percentage of apoptotic cells upon exposure to zwitterionic CDs at higher concentrations. Further, apoptosis was confirmed by significantlyincreased expression of pro-apoptotic gene (Bax) together with decreased expression of Bcl-2/Bax ratio. Collectively, this study suggests that zwitterionic CDs induce apoptosis in HEK 293 at higher concentration and the safe range for its intended application is found to be 50−200 μg/mL.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of CDs. (a) UV–vis image of CDs, (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different wavelengths, (c)Raman spectra of CDs showing D and G band, (d) FTIR spectra of the CDs.
15.7 mg/mL concentration of CDs in water were used for the characterization studies.
(a)Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs, (b) HR-TEM image, (c) Size distribution of CDs (d) AFM images of CDs (e) 3D image AFM image of CDs showing the height of CDs.

(a) Representative micrographs of zeta potential distribution of CDs, (b) Charge of CDs at different pH, (c) Zeta potential analysis depicting charge of the CDs, (d) representative image of ZCDs,(e) LCMS data of CDs.

Cell viability/cytotoxicity of HEK 293 cells exposed to various concentrations of CDs for 24 h, (a) MTT assay, (b) Neutral red assay, (c) ROS production of HEK 293 cells exposed to various concentration of zwitterionicCDsfor 24 h, (d-f) intracellular uptake of Zwitterionic CDs(blue) in HEK 293 cells after 24 h, data points are mean ± SD (n = 3), * indicates significant difference, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01 compared to control.

Representative fluorescent microscopic images of HEK 293cells exposed to ZwitterionicCDsfor 24 h, (a) assessment of mitochondrial membrane potential (JC1 staining), HEK 293 cells after exposure to ahigher concentration of zwitterion CDs (800,1600 μg mL−1) revealed decrease in red fluorescence indicating instability of mitochondria, while at 200,400 μg mL exhibits increased red fluorescence similar to control ensures the stability of mitochondria (b) Lysosomal integrity (acridine orange staining) in healthy cells can be visualized as orange–red fluorescing granules and cytoplasm remains green. Loss of lysosomal integrity, reduced red fluorescence and increased green fluorescence (1600 μg m L−1) (c) Cytoskeletal integrity assessment (Rhodamine-Phallodin staining), (d) Nuclear condensation (DAPI staining), smaller highly fluorescent nuclei with condensed chromatin indicate apoptosis.(Scale bar: 20 μm, magnification; 200x).

(a)The flow cytometric scatter plots of HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs by Annexin-V/PI staining, (b) Representative images of each quadrants respectively, necrotic cells (Q1), late apoptotic cells (Q2), healthy viable cells (Q3), early apoptotic (Q4). (c) The statistical data of the percentage of early and late apoptotic cells. Data points are expressed as Mean ± SD (n = 3). ***P < 0.001, **P < 0.01,*P < 0.05 compared to control.

mRNA expression levels for HEK293 cells after exposure to varying concentrations of zwitterionicCDsfor 24 h, (a) Bcl2, (b) Bax, (c) Bcl2/Bax ratio, (d) NF-kBexpression. Data points are mean ± SD (n = 3), * indicates significant difference at p < 0.05; **p < 0.01 compared to control.

head.ce:title: Mechanism of action and cellular responses of HEK293 cells on challenge with zwitterionic carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon, an extremely versatile element has great demand in the field of nanoscience. Carbon-based nanostructures are exponentially increased due to its wide range of applications in biotechnological and environmental approaches; hence, its safety assessment is of greater concern. In the present study, high quantum yielding zwitterionic carbon dots were synthesized, characterized and its safety assessment at different concentration ranges (50–1600 μgmL−1) on HEK 293 cells was carried out. Cellular, mitochondrial, lysosomal integrity and ROS generation were assessed using specific fluorochromes.The key cellular event apoptosis was assessed by annexinpropidium iodide staining using imaging flow cytometry. Moreover, the mRNA levels of the apoptotic genes were determined by real-time PCR. The results revealed that the cell viability assays (MTT, NR) and mitochondrial membrane potential were altered on exposure to a higher concentration of zwitterionic CDs for 24 h. Also, annexinpropidiumiodidestaining exhibited an increased percentage of apoptotic cells upon exposure to zwitterionic CDs at higher concentrations. Further, apoptosis was confirmed by significantlyincreased expression of pro-apoptotic gene (Bax) together with decreased expression of Bcl-2/Bax ratio. Collectively, this study suggests that zwitterionic CDs induce apoptosis in HEK 293 at higher concentration and the safe range for its intended application is found to be 50−200 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging subclass of nanomaterials, defined by characteristic sizes of <10 nm. CDs possess a carbon core that is functionalized by various groups at the surface. Carbon dots are considered as an epitome for various environmental, biological and energy-related applications. CDs with varying chemical structures have been actively produced through the optimization of synthesis, doping/surface modification and purification conditions.Increased permeability of CDs toward the cell membrane and even nuclear membrane during the long incubation make it potent reliable candidates for biomedical applications [1]. The key application of CDs includes bio-imaging, biosensing, therapeutic vehicles, and photocatalysis [2]. Bio-imaging of both cancerous and non-cancerous cell lines are well explored in different cell lines. The highly luminescent CDs are a potential class of biolabels due to their biocompatibility, low toxicity and can be mass-produced by a simple preparation method [3–6].

A great number of studies have been performed to synthesize CDs using different precursors; it may vary from green to inorganic precursors. Currently, a wide variety of materials have been explored for the synthesis of CDs and it ranges from organic compounds to the greener substrates. Initially, the synthesis was limited to carbonaceous materials. However, now many greener precursors ranging from food waste, peels, juices and even human residues have been explored for their potential in CD synthesis. These precursors are broadly classified into fruit source, food and beverages, animal derivatives, vegetables, waste materials, leaves and human derivative [7]. However, the CDs synthesized from the green source are generally excitation dependent with non-uniform size distribution. This could be because the greener source varies in composition widely and the surface functionalization depends on the type of greener precursor used. Also, the carbonization degree, which further depends on the type of synthesis method used, also affects the CDs synthesis and in turn its quantum yield. However, in contrast, according to the literature, high quantum yield CDs have been synthesized using organic compounds or organic precursors as it does not have many impurities and thus carbonization is also rapid [2,8].

The synthesis of CDs is relatively simple, low cost, and applicable to large scales. The existing approaches for synthesizing CDs include dry methods [9,10], laser ablation [11], solution methods combustion/thermal [12,13], electrochemical oxidation [14], organic synthesis [15], and microwave methods [16–18]. The microwave-assisted synthesis is a unique and simple technique which offers fast and efficient processing of materials with higher reproducibility. Also, it involves rapid, uniform heating and high purity over other conventional techniques [19].

It has been documented that most CDs are localized to the cell cytoplasm, including lysosomes, mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, and endoplasmic reticulum [20–22]. Size and surface charge are known to be the main determinants of cytoplasmic and nuclear uptake of nanoparticles [23–25]. Recent studies report that positively charged nanoparticles are mainly internalized by cells, whereas negatively charged ones after internalization interact with nuclei [26–28]. Besides, studies also report negatively charged CDs are found to be low in their fluorescent quantum yield compared to positive or neutral particles [29]. Along with that, the positively charged CDs are low in their biocompatibility [30].

It is well established that the zwitterionic surface provides a prospective platform for bioconjugation with different analytes devoid of any aggregation [31,32]. Hence, zwitterionic CDs with both positively and negatively charged functional groups can facilitate cytoplasmic uptake and subsequent nuclear translocation. A study reported that zwitterionic CDs with a quantum yield of 21.9 % are synthesized by a simple one-step synthesis method using β-alanine and citric acid [33]. In contrast, highly fluorescent, biocompatible zwitterionic CDs intended to monitor cancer in early stages have been synthesized with 80 % quantum yield [1]. Even though, numerous studies on the potential applications of CDs in the biomedical field, toxicity studies have been limited. CDs can cross the various biological barriers and may reach the most sensitive organs [34]. Similar to nanomaterial, most CDs are localized to the cell cytoplasm, including the lysosomes, mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, and endoplasmic reticulum [35]. Studies have also documented on the subcellular targeting features of surface-regulated CDs [36]. Hence, a detailed understanding of cellular interactions and intracellular distributions of CDs is essential.

In the present study, novel highly fluorescent zwitterionic CDs were synthesized by microwave pyrolysis and its toxicity assessment was carried out in HEK 293 cells.These CDs are intended to be used for drug delivery and bioimaging applications. The advantage of zwitterionic CDs in cancerous cell environment has been reported by sriet al., but its cellular fate and interaction on normal cells remain elusive. Zwitterionic carbon dots due to its small size it may reach potentially sensitive targets through blood and lymph circulation. The kidney is considered as the major secondary target organs in which nanoparticle toxicity should be thoroughly evaluated. The human embryonic kidney, HEK293 cells are specific cell lines commonly used in cell biology and toxicology studies [37]. Hence, in this study, the cellular response and interaction of synthesized zwitterion CDs on HEK 293 cells were assessed. An attempt also made to assess the cellular mechanism involved in response to zwitterionic CDs

All the chemicals used in the study were of analytical grade. Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM), Trypsin-EDTA was obtained from Gibco (Grand Island, NY, USA). 2,7dichlorofluorescein acetate (DCFH-DA) and JC1 were procured from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). DAPI, 4, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole and acridine orange was purchased from Hi-media Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). RhodaminePhalloidin obtained from Abcam.Neutral Red stain(SRL, Mumbai, India). RNA isolation kit wasprocured from Qiagen, Germany. cDNA synthesis (TB Green™ Premix Ex Taq ™ Kit) and RT PCR kit (Prime Script™ RT reagent Kit) purchased from Takara, Japan. Primers used in this study are obtained from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Alexa Fluor® 488 annexin V/propidium iodide (PI) detection kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA)

CDs were synthesized using as documented in our previous study [1]. In brief, CDs were synthesized using citric acid and l-cysteine as the precursor compound. The citric acid (1 g) and l-cysteine (25.2 mg) were dissolved in 10-ml water. The obtained transparent solution was kept in a domestic microwave oven (700 W, 3 m). The CDs formed were allowed to cool at room temperature, followed by the addition of distilled water (5 mL), a colloidal solution of CDs was obtained. Further, the colloidal solution was filtered by the membrane and thendialyzed against water using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da). The obtained solutionof CDs was then used for its characterization.

Synthesized CDs were characterized usingUV-visible spectrophotometry, zeta potential analysis, Raman spectra,Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy(FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and LC–MS analysis. UV–vis spectrophotometer (T90 + UV/VIS spectrometer) was used to characterize the zwitterionic carbon dots based on absorption maxima. Raman spectroscopy was done using Inspectr R532with 532 nm green laserfor obtaining Raman spectra of the synthesized CDs. FTIR spectra of CDs was done in the range 500-4000 cm−1 using Perkin–Elmer FT-IR spectrometer in ATR mode. The particle size of CDs was confirmed by TEM (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM-2200 FS, JAPAN), the zwitterionic nature of CDs was confirmed from the zeta potential using zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments). Along with this, the charge of zwitterionicCDswas investigated over a different pH range starting from pH, 1–12. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LCMS) data of CDs were analyzed by shcShine Biotech Pvt. Ltd. Faridabad, Haryana, India.

Human embryonic kidney cell line, HEK 293 (National Centre for Cell Science, India) was used for the experiment. Cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic solution.The cells were grown in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cellular response to zwitterionic carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: The effects of zwitterionic CDs on cell viability in HEK 293 cells were studied using MTT assay [38]. For this, the cells were seeded in 96-well plates (100 μL/well) with a seeding density of 10,000 cells/well and incubated for 24 h. Zwitterionic CDs at different concentrations (50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL−1) was added to the wells with cultured cells and further incubated for 24 h. Following incubation, MTT (3- (4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 was added to the assay wells and incubated for 4 h. The formazan product formed was dissolved in DMSO and absorbance was recorded at 540 nm using a multiwell plate reader (ELx 808,Bio-Tek Instruments, USA), Cells unexposed to zwitterionic CDs were taken as the control.

The neutral red uptake (NRU) assay, viability assay based on the ability of living cells to incorporate and bind neutral red (NR) [39]. Briefly, HEK 293 cells were seeded at a density of 10,000 cells per well in a 96-well plate for 24 h, followed by exposure to zwitterionic CDs at different concentrations (50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL−1) with cultured cells and further incubated for 24 h. Following incubation, neutral red solution (0.1 %) incomplete media was added to assay wells and incubated for 3 h in the dark. Neutral red lysis solution including acetic acid, ethanol and deionized distilled water (1:50:49) was then added to solubilize the lysosomal neutral red. The plates were gently shaken for 10 min and the absorbance was measured using a microplate reader (ELx 808, BioTek Instruments, USA) at 540 nm. Cells unexposed to zwitterionic CDs were used as the control.

The intracellular uptake of zwitterionic CDs was assessed on HEK 293 cells. For this, zwitterionic CDs at different concentrations (50, 100, 200 μg mL-1) were incubated with HEK 293 cells (1 × 10 [5] cells) seeded on coverslips in a 6-well plate. After incubation for 24 h, cells were incubated with acridine orange at a concentration of 5 μg mL-1 for 30 min and visualized using a fluorescence microscope (Axio Scope A1, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

The production of intracellular ROS was measured using the oxidation-sensitive fluorescent dye 2−7- dichlorofluorescindiacetate (DCFH-DA). Briefly, HEK 293 cells were seeded at a density of 10,000 cells per well in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h, followed by the addition of zwitterionic CDs at different concentrations (50,100, 200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL-1) and further incubated for 24 h. The cells were washed with PBS and DCFH-DA (5 μM/well) in DMEM was added to each well and incubated for 45 min under dark. Fluorescence was measured using a microplate reader (Synergy H1 Hybrid, BioTek Instruments, USA) with excitation and emission wavelength of 495 and 530 nm, respectively. 0.01 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in complete media was used as a positive control and cells without CDs was taken as the negative control. The results are presented as the percentage of fluorescence intensity relative to control wells.

Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was determined by JC-1 staining. JC-1, cationic fluorescent probe, is the most specific for assessing changes in MMP [40,41]. In healthy functional mitochondria, JC-1 forms increased J-aggregates (red) than monomers (green) and are detectable by fluorescence microscopy. Mitochondrial depolarization is indicated by a shift from red to green. Briefly, HEK 293 cells were seeded on coverslips in a 6-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well and incubated until 80 % confluence was attained. The cells were exposed to varying concentrations of zwitterionic CDs (200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL-1) in complete media for 24 h in a humidified environment at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Cells were washed with PBS and incubated in culture media containing a 5 μM concentration of JC-1 for 30 min. The cells were washed with PBS and observed under a fluorescence microscope (Axio scope A1, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

Lysosomal integrity was assessed by acridine orange staining [42]. AO, metachromatic fluorophore exhibits red fluorescence in intact lysosomes (acidic pH) and green in the cytosol (low acidic pH). The lysosome destabilization was observed based on the decrease in red fluorescence/increase in green fluorescence. HEK 293 cells were seeded in 6-well plates with coverslips at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. The cells were incubated in a humidified environment at 37 °C with 5% CO2 until 80 % confluence was attained. Followed by exposure to zwitterionic CDs (200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL-1) for 24 h, the cells were incubated with acridine orange (5 μg mL-1) for 30 min and visualized using a fluorescence microscope (Axio Scope A1, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

Cytoskeletal integrity of HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs was analyzed using rhodamine-phalloidin staining. HEK 293 cells were seeded on coverslips in a 6-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well and incubated in a humidified environment at 37 °C with 5% CO2 until the cells attain 80 % confluency. Further, the cells were exposed to (200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL−1) of zwitterionic CDs for 24 h, followed by a PBS wash and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (10 min). For permeabilization, 0.1 %Triton X-100 was added and incubated for 5 min. The cells were stained with rhodamine-phalloidine (100 nM), incubation for 30 min. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (Invitrogen) at a concentration (1 μg mL−1) and analyzed by a fluorescence microscope (Axioscope A1, Carl Zeiss, Germany.

Nuclear condensation or nuclear fragmentation is one of the hallmarks of apoptosis. Nuclear morphology was analyzed by DAPI staining. In brief, HEK 293 cells (1 × 105 cells mL−1) were grown on coverslips in a 6-well plate for 24 h. The cells were exposed to zwitterionic CDs (200, 400, 800, 1600 μg mL−1). Further, the cells were washed with PBS, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (10 min), permeabilization with 0.1 % Triton X-100 (5 min) and stained with DAPI (1 μg mL−1) for 5 min at room temperature. Micrographs of the stained HEK cells were observed under a fluorescence microscope (Axio scope A1, CarlZeiss, Germany).

Different modes of cell death, apoptosis/necrosis was assessed using Alexa Fluor® 488 annexin-V/propidium iodide (PI) detection kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific), following the manufacturer's instructions. For this, 1 × 105 cells were seeded in 6-well plates, allowed to adhere and exposed to different concentrations of zwitterionicCDsfor 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were harvested, incubated with Alexa Fluor® 488 annexin V/propidium iodide (PI) in the dark (15 min) according to manufacture guidelines. Following the incubation, samples were immediately analyzed via imaging flow cytometry (Amnisflowsight, France). Annexin V staining was detected as green fluorescence and PI as red fluorescence. Untreated cells served as a negative control. Samples were analyzed using amnis, Idea software

Total RNA was isolated from HEK 293 cell lines exposed to various concentrations of Zwitterionic CDs(200, 800 and1600 μg mL−1) for 24 h using RNA isolation kit (Qiagen, Germany) and subjected to reverse transcription (cDNA synthesis) using TB Green™ Premix Ex Taq ™ Kit (Takara) according to the guidelines of the manufacturer. Real-time PCR of the transcribed cDNA was performed using Prime Script™ RT reagent Kit (Takara) with specific primers. All samples were tested in triplicate. β-actin mRNA was used as the internal control and the data of target genes were normalized to β-actin mRNA by using the 2-ΔΔCT method [43]. The amplification conditions were optimized as follows: pre denaturation at 95 °C for 30 s, followed by 35 cycles of denaturation: 95 °C for 3 s, annealing: for 30 s and extension: 72 °C for 6–15 s. The following gene-specific primers were used for, Real-time polymerase chain reaction analysis.
1. Human Bax
Forward 5′-CTG CAG AGG ATG ATT GCC G-3′
Reverse 5′-TGC CAC TCG GAA AAA GAC CT-3′′
2. Human Bcl2
Forward: 5′-TCC CTC GCT GCA CAA ATA CTC-3′
Reverse: 5′-ACG ACC CGA TGG CCA TAG A-3′
3. Human NFκB
Forward: 5′-CGCCGCTTAGGAGGGAGA-3′
Reverse: 5′-AGGTATGGGCCATCTGCTGT-3′
4. Human β-actin
Forward: 5′-AAC TAC CTT CAA CTC CAT CA-3′′
Reverse: 5′-GAG CAA TGA TCT TGA TCT TCA-3

Data were expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical comparisons between control and zwitterionicCDsexposed groups were done using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’sposthoc test (SPSS ver 22, IBM Corp). The p values (p < 0.001, p < 0.01, p < 0.05) are considered as significant.

Various techniques have been used to characterize the size, crystal structure, elemental composition and various other physical properties of nanoparticles. In the present study, UV–vis spectrophotometry, zeta potential analysis, Raman spectra, FTIR, TEM, AFM and LCMS analysis were used to characterize the microwave-assisted synthesized zwitterionic CDs. The optical properties of CDs were determined by UV visible spectroscopy. Fig. 1 (a), depicts the UV visible spectra of the synthesized zwitterionic CDs. The UV absorption peak was obtained at 360 nm, is because of the n-π* transition of CO bonds. This n-π* is responsible for photoluminescence (PL) mechanism. The CDs were excited with different wavelengths ranging from 290 to 400 nm Fig. 1(b). Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis revealed the excitation independent emission, this behavior may be attributed to different emissive sites or more amine groups present on the surface of CDs [44].

Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the structural and electrical properties of zwitterionic CDs. Raman spectra of zwitterionic CDs exhibit two characteristic peaks, one at 1293 cm-1 and the other at 1435 cm-1 correspond to D and G band, respectively. Characteristic Raman spectra of zwitterionic CDs are represented in Fig. 1(c). Raman spectroscopy revealed that the D band is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of the disordered graphite. The G band is associated with the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice. The ratio of ID/IG is 0.99, which is a characteristic of the disorder extent and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon, implying the high luminescence of CDs could be due to surface defects. It has been documented that the intensity of the D band corresponds to more defects in the structure of CDs and may contribute toward fluorescence [45,46].The FTIR spectra of CDs showed hump at 3380 cm−1 which is assigned to the presence of OH in water or NH2 group in CDs. A broad peak corresponding to 2937 cm−1 due to the presence of CH3 groups at the CD surface. The peak corresponding to 2565 cm-1 and 1632 cm−1 shows numerous amine groups. The abundance of the carboxylic group is confirmed by sharp peaks at 1632 cm−1, 1405 cm−1 and 1205 cm−1. A peak at 561 cm−1 shows SO2 at the surface of CDs. These results show that the CD surface is rich in carboxylic, amine and sulphur groups (Fig. 1d). Results obtained were similar to that reported by Sri et al.,[1].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed thatthezwitterionic CDs were well dispersed (Fig. 2 a).The HR-TEM images visibly display that the CDs exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.206 nm (Fig. 2b), indicating CD crystallinity. The average size of CDs was calculated from (Fig. 2c) using ImageJ software. The average size calculated was 2.32 ± 0.17 nm with the particles showing a narrow size distribution from 1.5 to 4 nm. These results are similar to those reported by Sri et al.,[1].

Further Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) extends the three-dimensional analysis and visualization of zwitterionic CDs, Fig. 2 (d). The AFM data show that CDs are well dispersed in nature similar to the results obtained from TEM. The height profile of zwitterionic CDs is shown in Fig. 2 (e).The synthesized zwitterionic CDs were found to be very small ranging 2–5 nm.

The zwitterionic nature of CDs was confirmed from the zeta potential studies of CDs (Fig. 3 a). The overall charge of CDs was 0.00489, which is almost zero. However, as found earlier three charged species were present with different populations. The highest population (76.3 %) was of the particles with a negative charge (-12.0 mV). CDs with a charge of 31.6 mV have a population of 22.2 %. While only a few particles (1.5 %) were highly positive with a charge of 143 mV (Fig. 3b). The study on zwitterionic CDs on various pH, the results show that CDs are mostly zwitterionic over a wide pH range and are therefore more stable (Fig. 3 c). However, the charge state of CDs varies from zwitterionic to slightly cationic only at pH 3. Other than zwitterionic nature, the CDs exhibit slight anionic behavior. The slightly cationic and anionic behavior of zwitterionic CDs may be attributed to the non-uniform radical charge distribution. Fig. 3(d) shows the LCMS data, there are three prominent peaks at 196.28 m/z, 242.2 m/z and ∼416.11 m/z correspond to citric acid, Cysteine and C-Dot, respectively. The zwitterionic surface provides a non-fouling state and makes the CDs resistant to nonspecific binding [47,48]. The representative image of synthesized zwitterionic CDs is given in (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cellular response to zwitterionic carbon dots
Cell viability (MTT and Neutral red assays)
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability of zwitterionic carbon dots was assessed by biochemical endpoint assays MTT, NR uptake. MTT assay revealed the cellular viability of zwitterionic CDs on HEK 293 and it was reduced to almost 80 % at a higher concentration (1600 μg mL-1) compared to control (p < 0.05) (Fig. 4 a). In contrast, no significant difference in cell viability was observed in HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs at a concentration range (50–800 μg mL-1) compared to the control. More than 95 % viability was observed up to 800 μg mL-1. To confirm cell viability, neutral red (NR) assay was performed.The neutral red assay measures cell viability based on the uptake of neutral red by live cells. The percentage of viability of HEK 293 cells after exposure to zwitterionic CD for 24 h at different concentrations; 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, and 1600 μg/mL was 93.5 %, 98.7 %, 95.7 %, 95 %, 89.5 % and 77.2 %, respectively.Similar to MTT results, NR assay showed decreased viability compared to control on exposure to a higher concentration of zwitterionic CDs (1600 μg mL-1). NR data correlate well with the MTT results, both the assays show at a higher concentration of zwitterionic CDs induced reduction in cell viability. However, a concentration-dependent reduction in cell viability was observed in the NR assay (Fig. 4b). These results indicate zwitterionic CDs do not induce cytotoxicity at lower concentrations and induce cytotoxicity at higher concentrations. Recently, a study documented zwitterionic CDs showed low cytotoxicity with greater than 95 % cell viability at concentrations up to 500 μg mL-1 in human WI-38 cells and HeLa cell lines [33]. Similar results of decreased cytotoxicity were observed on zwitterionic CDs prepared from different substrates [49]. Nonetheless, a study on oral cancer cell lines documented that zwitterionic CDs induced reduction in cellular viability at a concentration of 400 μg mL-1(1). Recent studies have proven on the inaccuracy and less reliability of viability assays on cells treated with carbon nanomaterials [50,51]. Hence, further toxicological screening has to be carried out to validate the results.

The intracellular fate of nanoparticles and its safe entry into cells is a key step in achieving its therapeutic efficacy. The cellular uptake of zwitterionic CDs was observed on HEK 293 cells after 24 h of exposure. Visible blue fluorescence was observed in the cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs even at lower concentrations (50–200 μg mL-1) (Fig. 4 d, e, and f). The significant blue fluorescence in HEK293 cells indicating cellular entry of zwitterionic CDs. Studies have documented that cellular uptake of zwitterionic carbon dot depends on the timeand dose [1]. However, in this study, we only visualized the internalization of zwitterionic CDs.

Most of the cellular and biochemical alterations in the cells are due to ROS-mediated toxicity and several in vitro studies have documented the same [52]. It is established that increased ROS generation as an initiator factor of toxicity in nanomaterials exposed cells [53,54]. It is well established that reactive species of molecular oxygen (ROS) can induce cytotoxicity through oxidative stress. In this study, the levels of ROS in HEK 293 cells treated with zwitterionic CDs were assessed by the DCFH-DA assay. A concentration-dependent increase in ROS production was detected in zwitterionic CDs exposed HEK 293 cells (Fig. 3c). A significant increase in the production of ROS was observed at higher concentration (1600 μg mL-1) compared to control (p < 0.01).The DCFH-DA assay revealed concentration dependent increase in ROS levels. There is increasing evidence that ROS and mitochondria play a key role in the induction of apoptosis [55]. A similar study on zwitterionic CDs, documented at low concentration of 200 μg mL-1 negligible amount of ROS production in Fadu cells [1].

After the entry of nanoparticles inside the cell, they are mostly transported to different intracellular organelles, mitochondria and lysosomes are known to be the two main locations for nanomaterials [56,57]. Mitochondria play a prominent role in the generation of metabolic energy, responsible for the central reactions of oxidative metabolism. Mitochondrial dysfunction leads to alternation in membrane potential and oxidation–reduction potential of the mitochondria. Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), key in evaluating mitochondrial function [58,54]. Toxic insults may lead to alternation in MMP and promote cell death [59]. Normal or healthy mitochondria sustain, highly charged (negative inside) membrane potential [60]. In this study alternation of MMP mitochondrial membrane potential on exposure to zwitterionic CDs on HEK 93 cells was assessed using carbocyanine dye, JC1. JC-1 dye exhibits potential-dependent accumulation in mitochondria, in functional and polarized mitochondria, visible j aggregate formation or increased red fluorescence intensity was observed. In contrast, a shift in fluorescence intensity from red to green was noticed during membrane instability or membrane depolarization [61].

In this study, the membrane-permeant JC-1 dye staining revealed mitochondrial depolarization, indicated by a shift in fluorescence intensity from red to green or decrease in red/green fluorescence intensity at higher concentrations 800, 1600 μg mL-1 (Fig. 5 a). Increased red fluorescence was observed in similar to control cells in HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs 100 μg mL-1, 200 μg mL-1, 400 μg mL-1, indicating the retention of normal MMP and stability of mitochondria. Fig. 5a, depict that at higher concentration zwitterionic CDs (800, 1600 μg mL-1) induces alternation in MMP and may lead to cell death. Earlier studies have reported that the loss of MMP and disruption of mitochondria are associated with apoptosis by activating caspases and release of cytochrome c [54]. Further, it has been reported that nanoparticle-induced generation of ROS by depolarization of MMP [62].

Studies have reported on the visibility of nanoparticles in lysosomes upon its internalization and variety of nanomaterials associated with lysosomal dysfunction [63]. The interaction of zwitterionic CDs with the subcellular organelles lysosome and nucleus has been determined in this study. The lysosomal membrane permeability was assessed in HEK 293 cells exposed to different concentrations of zwitterionic CDs by acridine orange staining. Acridine orange staining revealed that lysosomes remain intact (red) with the cytosol (green) in control, similar observations are seen in cells exposed to zwitterionic CD concentration up to 400 μg mL-1. The destability of lysosome was observed at higher concentration (8,001,600 μg mL-1), with a slight decrease in red fluorescence/increase in green fluorescence (Fig. 5b). The lysosomal destabilization may trigger the mitochondrial pathway of apoptosis [64,65].

In the present study, morphological alternations of intracellular structures, nucleus and actin were also examined using fluorescent dyes. Actin filaments assign structural framework to the cell and it is involved in arranging the cellular contents, changing shape and cell movement. Nanoparticles can damage actin filaments that lead to loosening of the cytoskeletal integrity by inducing an alteration in mechanical properties and functions of the cell [66].It was observed an alternation in intracellular structures on exposure to a high concentration of zwitterionic CDs. The cytoskeletal integrity of the zwitterionic CDs exposed cells was analyzed by rhodamine-phalloidin staining. The actin networks (red) were labeled with fluorophore-conjugated phalloidin and the nuclei (blue) were labeled with DAPI. An alteration in the distribution of actin was observed in HEK 293 cells exposed to higher concentration (1600 μg mL-1) of zwitterionic CDs compared to control (Fig. 5c).

It has been reported that nanoparticles of size less than 10 nm act similar to a gas and can enter human tissues easily and may disrupt the cell normal biochemical environment [67]. Zwitterionic CDs, due to their small size may efficiently be transported to the nucleus. Hence, the interaction and effect of zwitterionic CDs on DNA were assessed. In the present study, nuclear condensation, the hallmark of apoptosis was assessed by DAPI staining. HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs (200 μg mL-1) for 24 h exhibit no visible chromosome condensation, similar to control. Non-homogeneously stained nuclei, chromatin condensation was observed in HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs at higher concentration (800, 1600 μg mL-1) (Fig. 5d). However, few apoptotic cells were also observed at 400 μg mL-This study confirms zwitterionic CDs are effectively transported to the nucleus by their zwitterionic surface charge and small size and it exerts nuclear condensation at higher concentration. This also reveals a concentration-dependent toxic effect of the zwitterionic CDs. Similarly, efficient transport of zwitterionic CDs to the nucleus has been reported [33].

Apoptotic cell death, highly regulated process involves several characteristic morphological and biochemical features, mainly cytoskeleton depolymerization, cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation and externalization of phosphatidylserine. In vitro and in vivo studies reported nanoparticle dose, cellular uptake and time of exposure are key factors of cellular apoptosis [68]. In view of the results obtained, zwitterionic carbon dots induced cell death was evaluated using annexin-V/propidium iodide staining. The alteration of the structure of the cellular membrane, resulting in exposure to the cell surface of anionic lipids, particularly of phosphatidylserine (PS), early after the onset of the execution phase of apoptosis [69]. Annexin V is a recombinant phosphatidyl serine-binding protein, strongly interacts with apoptotic marker phosphatidylserine is assessed by annexin/PI, double staining [70]. The percentage of viable cells (unstained cells), apoptotic (green, annexin positive), necrotic cells (red, PI-positive) and late apoptosis (dual stained cells) were analyzed, the representative images are shown in Fig. 6(a). Flow cytometry analysis revealed zwitterionic CDs induce apoptosis in HEK 293 cells and the percentage of apoptotic cells were significantly increased at higher concentrations (800 μg mL-1, 1600 μg mL-1), (p < 0.001) compared to the control. HEK 293 cells undergoing necrosis were found to be negligible.Similar to control (Fig. 6a-I) at low concentration of 200 μg mL-1 (Fig. 6a-II), increased the percentage of viable cells was observed.

Image flow cytometry analysis enabled to visualize the apoptotic morphology of single cells from the heterogeneous cell population [Fig. 6 (b, c)]. In this study, we aim at imaging flow cytometry (IFC), to observe cellular structures on a microscopic scale. The classical annexin V/propidium iodide staining along using image flow cytometer has been used in this study to distinguish different stages of cell death at the single-cell level. Fig. 5b shows representative single cells images of bright field, red and green fluorescence channels within a cell population. The healthy, early, late apoptotic and necrotic cells can be differentiated based on the morphology (bright field) and fluorescence.

Necrotic cells lost the integrity of the membrane visualized as red, PI-positive and annexin 488 (FITC) negative (Q1). Annexin 488 (FITC) /PI double positive, late apoptotic cells are shrunken with high intensity of PI staining (Q2). Healthy cells with intact cell membranes show both Annexin 488 (FITC) /PI negative (Q3). Early apoptotic cells are visualized as Annexin 488 (FITC) positive and PI negative (Q4). In this study, more early apoptotic cells were observed on exposure to a higher concentration of zwitterionicCDs. The induction of apoptosis was also concentration dependent, the percentage of apoptosis increases with increasing concentration of zwitterionic carbon dots.

It has been established that exposure to nanoparticles, both in vitro and in vivo systems primarily affects the expression of genes involved in the inflammatory response, apoptosis and oxidative stress. Also, the toxicogenomic studies found that nanomaterials exposure to cells may alter the genes involved in apoptosis [71]. To further assesses/investigate the mechanism of zwitterionic CDs induced apoptosis; the mRNA expression profile of key genes involved in the apoptotic pathway was evaluated. Proteins of the BCL2 family are well known to be the key regulator of the apoptotic pathway. Pro-apoptotic (Bax) and anti-apoptotic (Bcl-2) protein of bcl2 family regulates the mitochondrial-mediated intrinsic apoptotic pathway [72]. The results showed that Bax levels were significantly upregulatedcompared to control on exposure tozwitterionicCDs (800, 1600 μg mL-1) for 24 h (p < 0.01) [Fig. 7 (a–c)], togather with an increase in Bcl-2(800 μg mL-1).WhereasBcl-2/Bax expression ratio significantly decreased at higher concentrations (1600 μg mL-1), indicating apoptosis (p < 0.01). At lower concentrations, the (200 μg mL-1) expression levels of both bcl2 and bax were similar to that of control no alternation in the expression was observed. Also, assessed the role of NF-kB expression in HEK 293 cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs, upregulated expression of NF-kB observed on exposure to a higher concentration of zwitterionic CDs (1600 μg mL-1) for 24 h (p < 0.01). It is well documented that, a decrease in Bcl-2/Baxexpression ratio is associated with the induction of apoptosis [73,74]. In the present study, the Bcl-2/Bax ratio decreases with increasing zwitterionic CD concentrations, indicating apoptosis. Higher the Bax expression than that of Bcl-2, Bax-Baxhomodimers are formed and induce apoptosis. It has been well established that pro-apoptotic and anti-apoptotic proteins are the key regulators of programmed cell death and these proteins either protect or damage mitochondrial integrity by activating or inhibiting the release of downstream factors, such as cytochrome c (Cyt c), thus activating caspase-3 [75]. The pathway of apoptosis differs depending on the nanomaterials chemical nature [59].

Nuclear factor-kappa B, ubiquitous transcription factor plays a key role in inflammatory and immune responses and in regulating the expression of genes related to cell survival, proliferation and differentiation [76]. Additionally, in vitro studies have documented that; carbon nanoparticles can interfere with the NF-kB pathway due to its ability to induce oxidative stress [77–79]. Nuclear factor-kappa B (NF-kB), a redox-sensitive transcription factor, is activated by various apoptotic stimuli and may regulate the expression of pro-apoptotic or anti-apoptotic members of the Bcl-2 protein family [80,81]. The results of the study indicated that increased expression of NF-kB on exposure to a higher concentration of zwitterionic CDs. However, it was observed that NF-kB may regulate the expression of either pro-apoptotic or anti-apoptotic members of the Bcl-2 protein. However, no conclusive results obtained on its role in regulating the expression levels of Bcl-2 proteins in HEK cells exposed to zwitterionic CDs.

Among various nanoparticles, small carbon nanoparticles (CDs) are gaining importance due to its application in biomedical research and targeted drug delivery. Toxicological behavior of nanomaterial’s and its cellular fate is of major concern for its safety assessment. In this study, novel highly fluorescent, cost-effective zwitterionic CDs was synthesized and a systematic approach has been made to assess the cellular response and interaction of zwitterionic CDs on HEK 293 cells. In conclusion, at higherconcentration zwitterionic carbon dots induce mitochondrial dysfunction, and induction of ROS and nuclear condensation. In addition, mRNA expression profiles of pro- and anti-apoptotic genes of zwitterionic CDs induce apoptosis at higher concentrations via an intrinsic mitochondria-mediated pathway. We provide here the first evidence on the cellular interaction and mechanism of action of the zwitterionic CDs on HEK 293 cells. Further studies have to be conducted for the in-depth understanding of the mechanism of toxicity and its associated pathways. The knowledge on the toxicity of zwitterionic CDs may deliver researchers to develop further biological application of zwitterionic CDs in the future.
